data.title: Iamgold Stock: Limited Margin Of Safety At Current Levels
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day.

Q2 Production & Sales

IAMGOLD Corporation (NYSE:IAG) released its Q2 and H1-24 results in August, reporting quarterly production of ~166,000 ounces of gold, a 55% increase from the year-ago period. This was helped by another solid quarter out of Essakane (~111,000 ounces) and a far better quarter from its smaller Westwood Mine which produced ~35,000 ounces on the back of higher throughput and grades with significantly more underground ore tonnes mined. Finally and just as importantly, Cote Gold enjoyed its first quarter of meaningful production, producing ~20,000 attributable ounces for Iamgold (~35,000 ounces on 100% basis) as it continues to ramp to a goal of 90% of nameplate capacity at year-end.

Digging into the results a little closer, its largest Essakane Mine produced ~111,000 ounces at AISC of $1,481/oz, a material improvement from ~88,000 ounces at $1,587/oz in Q3-23. This was related to much higher grades (1.46 vs. 1.11 G/T of gold) that offset slightly lower throughput of ~2.97 million tonnes, with grades benefiting from positive reconciliation and in Phase 5. Notably, AISC actually declined year-over-year despite moderate inflationary pressures that continue to be felt sector-wide and despite higher sustaining capital spend in the period. Lower costs were related to the much higher denominator in Q2-24 and Iamgold increased Essakane’s FY2024 guidance on the back of its solid H1-24 performance, up from 350,000 ounces at the mid-point to 395,000 ounces.

On a negative note, Essakane won’t see a repeat of its strong H1-24 performance (~229,000 ounces) with lower grades and by extension higher costs expected in the second half of the year. Additionally, security in Burkina Faso continues to be an issue, with Iamgold stating that “terrorist-related incidents are still occurring in the country, the immediate region of the Essakane Mine.” This is obviously not ideal from either a supply chain, general safety and exploration standpoint. Like Boungou which also had a difficult time growing reserves as its footprint was limited because of security concerns, this is an unfortunate headwind relative to safer jurisdictions. There, the higher gold price is creating a lower hurdle regarding exploring for ounces that might have been otherwise been a little further from the plant and mine site.

In fact, as of 2023, the Sahel Region accounted for nearly half of global terrorism deaths, with the most recent significant incident near Essakane being at the Essakane Village Church near the mine in Q1-24. The silver lining for Iamgold at Essakane as an operator is that it continues to sport a phenomenal safety record on site. It is fortunately within its control. While security costs continue to rise, it’s much easier to absorb these elevated security costs relative to other mines in a record gold price environment with H1-24 mine-site free cash flow increasing nearly 200% year-over-year to ~$154 million.

Moving to its smaller Westwood Mine in Canada, production came in at ~35,000 ounces of gold, an 80%+ increase from the year-ago period. Higher production benefited from higher throughput and grades, with ~302,000 tonnes processed at 3.92 G/T of gold vs. ~251,000 tonnes at 2.53 G/T of gold in the year-ago period. Increased grades related not only to a meaningful bump in open-pit grades, but also significantly more ore tons mined underground at higher grades, with ~89,000 tonnes mined at an average grade of 9.22 G/T of gold. Not surprisingly, this significant increase in production and sales benefited unit costs, with AISC dropping to a more respectable figure of $1,663/oz vs. $2,903/oz in the year-ago period. In fact, given the higher gold price, Westwood actually generated mine site free cash flow in Q2-24.

Finally, its new Cote Gold Mine in Ontario (60.3% owned with Sumitomo, increasing to 70% owned by year-end) produced ~34,000 ounces of gold on a 100% basis in Q2-24 or ~20,000 ounces attributable to Iamgold. This was the first meaningful quarter of production for the asset and commercial production was declared in early August, with the mine processing ~834,000 tonnes at 1.39 G/T of gold at 90% recoveries. Notably, operating results improved further in Q3-24 despite the 8-day shutdown in September, with throughput doubling to ~1.63 million tonnes at a grade of 1.41 G/T of gold and 93% recoveries, representing ~41,000 ounces attributable to Iamgold.

Unfortunately, Iamgold noted Cote is expected to come in at the lower end of annual guidance, partially offsetting the guidance increases at its legacy operations. Still, this would imply production of at least 120,000 ounces in Q4-24 or 75,000+ ounces attributable to Iamgold, driving a major step change in Iamgold's consolidated gold production. As it stands, throughput is tracking well to meet the 90% mark exit rate goal for year-end, averaging 30,000 TPD for the first half of October or 83% of nameplate capacity of 36,000 TPD. Just as importantly, the autonomous haulage fleet (18 autonomous haul trucks in operation currently) at Cote is performing as expected, with Iamgold's stating the following in the Q2-24 call (emphasis added):

"So, I honestly do not see any issues with them (autonomous haulage fleet) and hopefully over time we could be even better and with our drilling automation as well. So working hard on both, but autonomous is absolutely working beautifully and not to underestimate as well the health and safety aspect of not having operators behind the steering. It's not about cutting job, it's really about improving, modernizing our operations and make it safer. But so far so good, excellent. I'll give a very high score to the implementation of autonomous haulage. I will redo it anytime."

— Iamgold Q2-24 Conference Call.

These comments echo Newmont's (NEM) satisfaction with its autonomous haulage fleet at its massive Boddington gold-copper mine in Australia, which was the first to have a fully operational open-pit mine with autonomous haulage.

As for other key metrics at Cote, Iamgold has noted that reconciliation is performing relatively well relative to the reserve model year-to-date in terms of its grade control model vs. reserve model (5% less ounces with fewer tonnes in grade control model but slightly higher grades). On a positive note, its milling and leaching circuits have shown the ability to operate at or above design capacity, and recovery has been slightly above plan to date. The latter is an encouraging sign for potentially getting more production out of this mine vs. its previous LOMP with further optimization and a potential increase to 40,000+ TPD.

This would be a bonus given that it not only increases Tier-1 jurisdiction ounces and Iamgold's ultimate multiple, but also helps to offset the production cliff at Essakane. It may not stay in production much past 2029 and has a declining grade profile over its remaining mine life.

As for Iamgold's sales, revenue spiked to $385.3 million in Q2-24 and operating cash flow soared to $160.7 million on the back of record gold prices. This was a material improvement from the year-ago period, benefiting from higher production/sales as well as stronger gold prices and lower than expected unit costs. Notably, Iamgold also shared that it expects to collect $27.3 million related to insurance in H1-24 from the Westwood stoppage in 2020 (seismic event). If the Bambouk asset sale can finally close, this would help to deleverage further vs. its Q2-24 ending net debt balance of ~$495 million.

Costs & Margins

Moving to costs and margins, Iamgold's AISC margins improved materially in Q2-24 to $677/oz, benefiting from lower AISC of $1,617/oz (Q3-23: $1,912/oz) and a record average realized gold price of $2,294/oz. This allowed the company to enjoy positive free cash flow despite ~$90 million in capex related to Cote in Q2-24. The continuation higher in the gold price has taken worries about any further share dilution off the table after a recent raise of ~72 million shares at US$4.17. Overall, this should be a gigantic sigh of relief for investors, given that that things were looking a lot more dire ahead of the Rosebel asset sale with a significantly over-budget build at Cote. In fact, Iamgold is set to generate well over $350 million in free cash flow next year, even using very conservative gold price assumptions.

So, how does the valuation look today after Iamgold's outperformance?

Valuation

Based on ~575 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$6.10, Iamgold trades at a market cap of ~$3.5 billion and an enterprise value of ~$4.0 billion. This is a massive increase from when its current CEO Renaud Adams (previously New Gold, Richmont Mines) joined Iamgold in April 2023. The impressive growth in market cap can be attributed to higher gold prices, the successful build of Cote which has transformed the company’s jurisdictional profile (now primarily Canada vs. previously being primarily in West Africa) and a far better job managing expectations. In fact, Iamgold has had one of the better track records among its peer group of beating guidance since Renaud Adams joined as CEO, a welcome departure from the previous period with constant misses and consistent disappointment.

That said, although increased trust in management related to better execution and bringing a new Tier-1 jurisdiction and Tier-1 mine online certainly justify an upside re-rating higher market cap, much of this is looking priced into the stock short-term. In fact, Iamgold is now trading at a higher enterprise value than B2Gold, a larger producer that is also undergoing a transformation from a jurisdictional standpoint as it works to bring Goose online in Canada. This does not mean that Iamgold can’t continue to head higher. Cote Gold will be a free cash flow machine based on IAG’s 70% interest even if gold just hangs out at $2,400/oz, let alone where it sits atop $2,700/oz currently.

However, ~50% of attributable production is still coming from Burkina Faso, a less friendly jurisdiction that isn't afraid to raise royalty rates in periods of gold price increases and one that is quite challenging from a security standpoint.

Plus, even if IAG has upside to its updated fair value estimate of US$7.10, stocks rarely go up in straight lines and miners especially love to correct when everyone least expects it. As it stands, IAG is now running into its first area of key resistance since its 2022 bottom. So, with the stock lacking an adequate margin of safety and in a more vulnerable position technically with a less favorable reward/risk setup, I would be using this rally above US$6.10 as an opportunity to book some profits into strength if I were overweight the stock.

Summary

Iamgold has been one of the best performers year-to-date, adding well over $1.5 billion in market cap and just coming off the declaration of commercial production at its massive Cote Gold Mine in Ontario, Canada. This asset (70% interest) is transformational for the company, especially with souring sentiment on Burkina Faso and West Africa in general. However, while the rising tide in the form of the gold price has lifted all boats, IAG has found itself vulnerable to a correction after its near-parabolic year-to-date rally and looking closer to fairly valued short-term.

Hence, while I think IAG makes a solid buy-the-dip candidate under better leadership with Renaud Adams, I continue to see far more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector where I remain focused currently. Additionally, if I held an overweight position in IAG stock, I see this as an opportunity to book some profits above US$6.10.

data.title: Red Robin's Burger Burned: Can New Activist Investors Turnaround The Company?
data.description:
Red Robin Gourmet Burgers, Inc. (NASDAQ:RRGB) has experienced a significant decline in the share price of 45% YTD, reflecting the headwinds the company is currently facing, including rising costs and declining customer visits.

However, since the announcement of the agreement between JCP Investment Management and Jumana Capital Investments, the share price has doubled.

As a result, shareholders will be paying close attention to the upcoming earnings release on November 6, looking for any strong signs of a turnaround, including a clear long-term plan from both the funds and management.

Despite the low valuation ratios following the steep drop in share price, I rate this stock as a Sell.

The fundamentals paint a challenging picture, with rising costs outpacing revenue growth and an increasing reliance on onetime gains from sale leaseback to mitigate losses.

For now, I would like to start with a brief company overview, for those readers new to this stock.

Company Overview

Red Robin operates a chain of 411 restaurants in 39 states (in addition to 92 franchised restaurants across 14 states) offering gourmet burgers, among other American-style cuisine.

They operate in one business segment. However, the company does provide a breakdown of revenue in their earnings reports. I considered including a snapshot of the latest 10Q below.

Ownership-wise, the latest 14A shows 3.19% of total beneficial ownership among all 14 directors and executive officers.

The president and CRO, G.J. Hart, owns 1.03% of the common stock. In my view, management doesn't have enough skin in the game.

As a side note, I (generally) favor companies where management owns over 20% of the common stock.

Price Action

I'd like to start my analysis by looking at the recent price action.

A quick look at the daily chart below shows the sheer decline of 80% since March 2021.

If we look at the YTD performance, the share price declined by over 45%, showcasing the pessimism surrounding this stock. So, it's no surprise that the performance chart below shows a big drop compared to the rest of the restaurant industry.

Let's dive deeper into the fundamentals to understand what has been going on over the last few years.

Fundamentals

A quick look at the income statement shows an interesting pattern, with revenue increasing since 2020. The chart below shows the YoY variation.

It looks like revenue has been going up, but gross margins haven't followed the same trend.

The decline in gross profits is especially concerning in 2024, with a 27% decline in Q1 2024, followed by a 4.4% decrease in Q2 2024.

If we look at the drivers behind this decline in the first quarter, we will find a 270 bps increase in labor costs due to elevated workers' compensation and group health insurance costs, and a 70 bps increase in occupancy costs due to the impact of higher fixed rents following the sale leaseback transactions of 28 restaurant locations​.

Besides rising costs, revenue also dropped as the number of guests decreased by 8.3% YoY.

In short, fewer people are coming to the restaurants, and at the same time, costs are going up. Not a good sign, in my view.

Let's look now at the profitability and bottom line of the company.

As shown above, the company has been operating at a loss every quarter, consecutively, since Q2, 2023.

The net loss, especially in Q1 this year, has been amplified due to the absence of a one-time gain from sale leasebacks in the same period last year, which generated a $14.6 million gain, further widening the gap between the two periods.

However, what concerns me is that in the first quarter of 2024, there was a significant transaction involving the sale-leaseback of 10 restaurant properties. This transaction resulted in a gain of $7.4 million, net of expenses​.

If the company hadn't engaged in sale-leasebacks at the beginning of this year, the decline would have been even worse. As a side note, in Q2, there haven't been any sale-leasebacks.

In my view, it seems that the company is using the proceeds from sale-leasebacks to mitigate their net losses.

Let's move on to the balance sheet.

Since October 2023, there has been a significant decline in cash per share.

However, on the bright side, net debt has been declining as well, although at a slower rate.

By the end of Q2, the company had $23.1 million in cash and equivalents, but with current liabilities at $189.7 million, it looks like the company is under financial strain.

The liquidity ratios show a dire picture, with more current liabilities than current assets.

Liquidity Ratio Value
Current Ratio 0.45
Quick Ratio 0.30
Cash Ratio 0.12

So, how are they paying their liabilities?

Well, for now, the company is using operating cash flow to cover immediate liabilities. In the first half of fiscal 2024, Red Robin generated $14.2 million in net cash from operating activities, although I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne yet as this represented a decrease from the $18.2 million in H1 2023.

In addition, the company still has $25 million available to borrow from its credit facility, but they've already taken out $162.3 million.

Finally, as already mentioned, the company might be using the cash inflows from the sale-leasebacks to repay debt and cover expenses.

Insider/Activist/Institutional Buying Activity

A quick look at the recent insider buying activity shows two modest operations (<$100k) by the CEO and the CFO in September this year.

However, what I find remarkable is the $450k buy from JCP Investment Management, which increased their stake by 28%, adding up to 7.9% of the common stock, and the $437k buy from Jumana Capital Investments, which now owns 3.7% of the common shares.

However, my investment strategy tends to steer clear of private equity firms when a company is struggling, regardless of their intentions, or long-term vision.

To be clear, I would have much preferred a change in management or substantial insider buying over the involvement of these two firms.

I'm also concerned about the 62% drop in ownership by Archon Capital Management in September and the 76% decrease by Blackrock in July.

On top of that, earlier this year, both Divisadero Street Capital Management and T. Rowe Price Investment Management sold all their positions in the company.

Valuation

It shouldn't be any surprise to see low valuation ratios, considering the sheer decline in the share price over the last couple of years.

Most of the valuation metrics are trading close to the 5-year minimum.

However, if we compare the company's EV/EBITDA to its peers, the company doesn't seem to be cheap.

The table below is arranged in descending order by EV/EBITDA.

Additionally, Red Robin has the maximum 1-year, and also 5-year, maximum drawdown among its peers.

Why I Can Be Wrong

In my view, the main factor that could break my bear thesis for this stock is a turnaround due to the involvement of activist investors and PE firms, particularly JCP Investment Management and Jumana Capital Investments, respectively.

The agreement reached between these two investment firms on September 23, 2024 shows their commitment to turnaround the company, by seeking changes in corporate governance (which is something I would highly favor), influencing management decisions, or advocating for new strategic initiatives.

Additionally, the company seems to have made recent changes to its menu by adding a new spicy menu in September this year.

If this proves to attract more customers to dine in, then their revenue could improve in the next few quarters. Additionally, gross margins could improve as well if PE firms implement cost-cutting measures, including significant lay-offs.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I give Red Robin a Sell rating.

Looking at the recent performance, it is clear that the company is struggling with rising costs and fewer customers dining in.

Despite their revenue going up over the last years, it's not translating into better profits.

Additionally, they've been increasingly relying on selling properties to stay afloat, which isn't a long-term fix.

Even though activist investors and private equity firms have stepped in, I haven't seen yet any real changes that would make me feel confident about a turnaround. Additionally, management's North Star plan, introduced in 2023, is still not showing any positive signs of value improvement, considering the significant decline in the share price over the past year.

Until Red Robin shows it can cut costs and bring in more customers, I think the stock has more downside. For now, I believe it's best to steer clear.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IMCG: Growth ETF Without Mega Caps
data.description:
This article series aims at evaluating ETFs (exchange-traded funds) regarding past performance and portfolio metrics. Reviews with updated data are posted when necessary.

IMCG strategy

iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:IMCG) was launched on 06/28/2004 and tracks the Morningstar US Mid Cap Broad Growth Index. It has 301 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.78% and an expense ratio of 0.06%. Morningstar defines Mid-Cap stocks as falling between the 70th and 90th percentile of the market capitalization of the Morningstar US Market Extended Index. As described by iShares, the underlying index selects stocks "issued by companies that typically have higher than average historical and forecasted earnings, sales, book value and cash flow growth."

The fund's turnover rate was 43% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the S&P Mid-Cap 400 index, represented by iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH).

IMCG portfolio

The heaviest sectors in the portfolio are industrials (25% of asset value) and information technology (20.1%). Other sectors are below 12%. Compared to the benchmark, IMCG mostly overweights technology and communication. It significantly underweights financials, energy and materials.

The portfolio is very diversified and risks related to individual companies are low. Indeed, the top 10 holdings (listed below) represent only 10.2% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs 1.55%.

Performance
