IMCG has the cheapest expense ratio. It is in third position for size (assets under management) and in liquidity (dollar volume). The next chart plots total returns, starting on 12/14/2016 to match all inception dates. IMCG is the best performer ahead of FNY, while IVOG is lagging.

Nonetheless, IMCG is in the middle of the pack over the last 12 months:

Takeaway

iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IMCG) holds 301 mid-cap stocks selected based on historical and forecasted growth metrics. The portfolio is well-diversified, with a focus on industrials and technology. IMCG has outperformed a mid-cap benchmark since 2004, and six other mid-cap growth ETFs since 2016. Moreover, its expense ratio is cheap. IMCG is a good pick for investors seeking exposure to growth without the usual mega-cap companies. Nonetheless, keep in mind that IMCG is volatile and has lower risk-adjusted performance than the benchmark.

data.title: AT&T Q3 Earnings: A Good Quarter, But There Are Caveats
data.description:
AT&T Q3 Earnings Review

After Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) reported its third quarter results yesterday and the stock fell by more than 5% on the day, the share price response to AT&T Inc.'s (NYSE:T) Q3 earnings appears to be radically different.

On the surface, the quarter was not very different from that of Verizon - a slight beat on earnings and a slight miss on revenue. The positive share price reaction, however, suggests that there is more going on underneath the surface.

But before we jump into all the details of this quarter, we should acknowledge that AT&T's turnaround seems to have gathered momentum in 2024. So far, the stock has returned more than 36% on a year-to-date basis and is just inches away from T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS).

AT&T's relative performance to its peers should not come as a surprise, as the company seems to have already moved on from its fiasco of capital misallocation related to DirecTV and WarnerMedia. Although such events tend to linger for long with investors, back in 2021 I warned that these developments should not weigh on sentiment.

Then in October 2023, I took a deep dive into AT&T's business and showed why the "nightmare" for shareholders is finally over. Even though I wasn't trying to time the market, the analysis coincided with the stock's multi-year bottom when it was trading just slightly above $15 a piece.

So far, it appears that AT&T's management continues to make the right steps towards turning the business around by focusing on mobility in its home market, as opposed to other ventures outside its core business. The third quarter results seem to confirm this trend, albeit I wouldn't read too much into it when it comes to extrapolating medium and long-term trends.

Optimism Is Back

While AT&T's third quarter results appeared quite similar to those of Verizon's yesterday when we look at EPS and revenue numbers relative to expectations, AT&T fared far better from an operational perspective.

Postpaid phone subscribers' growth came in slightly higher than the one reported in Q2 2024, which helped dispel some fears that growth will soon stall to zero.

Growth in postpaid phone average revenue per user ("ARPU") was not spectacular during the quarter, but Fiber ARPU noted a large jump to above $70. This trend is likely to continue, as AT&T's management also reported an intake Fiber ARPU of around $75.

Although the share of wireline revenues is small in comparison to mobility, these trends will likely allow AT&T to continue to increase its margins going forward.

In comparison to a year ago, EBITDA margins improved both in mobility (from 55.9% in Q3 2023 to 57.4% this quarter) and in consumer wireline (from 31% in Q3 2023 to 32.8% in the current quarter). This expected improvement in margins was one of the key points that I made a year ago.

In terms of FY 2024 guidance, there weren't any changes, and it is quite similar to one of its major peers - Verizon (see below).

However, we should keep in mind that AT&T is smaller than Verizon in terms of revenue, which shows that the former will invest more in capex this year relative to its sales.

Given the expected adjusted EPS figures for 2024, it appears that AT&T is now trading at a notable premium to its competitor after the two stocks moved in opposite directions over the past month or so. This is to be expected given the slight divergence in operational performance between the two companies during the most recent quarter. However, a quarter does not set up a trend, and so far, I don't see a reason for a major divergence in the valuation of these two companies.

The Two Elephants In The Room

Despite the relatively good operational performance during the quarter, developments related to the company's dividend and debt remain as the key considerations for most investors.

After years of being the highest-yielding stock in its peer group, AT&T's dividend yield has now fallen below that of Verizon and the stock presently trades at a yield of slightly above 5%.

To an extent, this is related to the recent changes related to WarnerMedia, but it is also closely related to AT&T's better share price performance than that of Verizon. When evaluating the safety of the dividend, things do not look pretty on a GAAP basis, with the annual net income figure being subject to wild variations.

Of course, we should account for the non-cash impairment charges recently, which have a significant impact on the final net income figure. Both 2020 and 2022 results were impacted by such impairments and just as things looked as if there would be no more such charges, AT&T's management reported yet another round of roughly $4.4bn of impairments and restructuring charges.

These charges are related to assumptions regarding the company's legacy services and higher-than-expected operating expenses.

Operating expenses increased primarily due to a $4.4 billion non-cash goodwill impairment in the current quarter associated with our Business Wireline unit based on faster than previously anticipated industry-wide secular decline of legacy services. Also contributing to higher operating expenses was accelerated depreciation on wireless network equipment associated with our Open RAN network modernization efforts, and our continued network upgrades.

Source: AT&T 10-Q SEC Filing (emphasis added).

The market has mostly ignored these impairments as they relate to goodwill recorded as a result of previously made acquisitions. Nonetheless, they do send a signal to investors that returns on investments are likely to suffer as management assumptions about the future change. Moreover, even if we adjust AT&T's net income figure for all these non-cash charges (after tax), we end up with lower dividend coverage in comparison to FY 2023.

With AT&T's lower dividend yield, this is not a very positive sign for dividend-oriented investors.

In terms of debt, AT&T's management seems to be focused on the current capex cycle and the higher fiber investments expected in the coming year. As a result, debt repayments are not a priority currently, and that's why we see net debt falling down by low single digits.

At the same time, however, interest expense increased marginally during the quarter from a year ago, which highlights the risks associated with such large debt levels at a time when interest rates are rising.

Given the slight improvements in adjusted EBITDA, AT&T's leverage ratio is improving, albeit not at the rate needed in the current environment to reduce interest expenses and dispel the debt-related fears of investors.

Conclusion

AT&T Inc. reported a relatively good quarter, but nothing material to justify a change in narrative. From an operational standpoint, T appears to be doing slightly better than Verizon, but investors should not get carried away by quarterly results. The valuation and dividend yield gap between the two stocks is widening, and this could ultimately favor VZ shareholders.

data.title: Hedging Your Bets (And Your Sanity) In The Stock Market Casino
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Trading Gains as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

The Setup

The major equity indexes have become very extended after a virtually non-stop rally since October 2022. With indexes getting close to very long-term upside targets (like 6,000 for the S&P), one could argue the end is nigh. Or, at least a medium-sized wreck-oning of trading accounts. I’ll spare you the doomsday prophecy, since there is already an internet full of dead bears and delusional bulls.

Overall, hedging is the most important thing you can do for your trading account to manage risk. It also helps you sleep at night. If you can’t sleep, your position size is too large or the asset class you are trading (like futures…) might not be for you. The whole object of trading markets is to make money, right? If you can make money consistently without introducing unnecessary risk into your portfolio, you are on the right path. Home runs are for big risk-takers. Get rich over time. The intent of this article is to present several hedging strategies that can help your portfolio weather any storm that makes market-fall. Hopefully, you learn something you didn’t know before and can use it to your advantage!

How to Partition an Account: Basic Investing & Speculating 101

Depending on your level of experience trading markets, you can distill most things down to position size, diversification of a portfolio, and patience. It is a great exercise to chunk out your available capital into easy to track “units”. For a 250k account, 10k per unit would be appropriate for the average person. With a smaller dollar account of 5k, $200 per unit would scale the same. This is not advice, it’s an example. Your risk tolerance should dictate how much is too much.

When choosing a stock or ETF, I’ve found that no more than 3 units of available capital per position gives you flexibility when things go bad. You should always have some capital ready to deploy for a hedge if needed. It’s just good practice to have rainy day funds. If you have 3 units of a 3x leveraged ETF like TQQQ (30k with a 250k account size), the profits can be amazing. However, the unrealized losses could be equally amazingly bad if you chose the wrong horse. But, you can still profit from the hedge!

Types of Hedge Strategy

  1. Diversification - Easiest
  2. Add, Release, Reset - Easy
    • Using inverse ETFs with no leverage
    • Using inverse ETFs with leverage (2x, 3x, etc.)
  3. Options contracts - Hard
  4. Futures contracts - Turbo Bankrupt

Strategy #1: Diversification

This strategy is pretty easy since they teach you this in school and every financial advisor mentions it like they invented the term. If your position sizes are reasonable, diversification happens naturally when trading.

For example, using the above-mentioned 250k account size and 10k slices of it as “units”, a long stock position in SPY at max would be an allocation of 30k. For simplicity, let’s ignore price as a factor. This article is about the concepts. 30k long SPY and 30k QQQ is a big chunk of change for the whole portfolio. However, with buying both indexes, you have exposure to all tech and different sectors of the market that comprise the S&P. Just that allocation alone is diversified by default.

Let’s add, on top of that, a single stock like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). If you are trying to buy low, easing in by adding 1 unit of capital at a time will also reduce risk. If the price heads lower the next day, you can always add to improve the cost basis or wait longer. Normally, markets have a theme each day. Either it’s a trend, consolidation, or a face melter selloff kind of day. Markets don’t usually change trend in the middle of the day.

One thing I hear often is traders jumping the gun and going long or short too early. You can avoid pain here by easing into a position and scaling it when the price reaches the level you think is right for a buy/sell. Really, what I’m trying to say is… what’s the rush? Have patience when entering a position and do your research.

Strategy #2: Add, Release, Reset

Overall, the concept here is to add downside protection (using ETFs, options, etc.) for a few days while markets blow off some steam. Being quick to take short profits in a bull market is wise. However, if you take profits too early, you can always reset the hedge trade if markets continue to decline.

The SPY chart below is an example of using inverse ETFs to hedge a portfolio. In the example, SPDN (x1) and SPXS (x3) will be used. This methodology will apply to both leveraged hedges and non. The main assumption is we know the market is pulling back.

One very important practice that has saved my bacon many times is to set a stop order for a hedge before you get up from your computer. Get into the habit of “trust your gut but CYA”.

Step 1: Buy some shares of SPDN x1, or SPXS x3. It’s always your choice how much of a hedge to add. Some traders fully hedge longs 1:1, some only hedge 50% in an uptrend. It depends on your risk tolerance.

Step 2: After the market declines, and it looks like support is building, close the short position and pocket the loot. In theory, your portfolio should be intact after being stuck in neutral for a while.

A savvy trader would use the short profits and add to long positions to improve the cost basis. Compounding is a powerful tool in your trading quiver. Some of my best trades have been short-term hedges. The trick is to not to over-trade and let the market develop. It will reduce the temptation to take profits too early. It’s when you close too early and reset shorts at a lower price, it can lead to trouble. Mentally, it is easier to give up some short profits on a solid bounce if you have a great entry, vs. having to chase the market down. Then, over-trading is a common cause of insomnia and blown accounts.

You can repeat the whole process on a good bounce. Try drawing a box around the consolidation zone once there is a clear first bottom. Now that you have a range drawn, a breach above or below is the signal for a continuation of trend, or confirmation of trend change. The first leg down is always the most difficult to forecast where it will stop.

Step 3: This step is the danger zone. A small and easy correction can be fun to trade. A continued market beatdown can be infuriating to trade. This example using SPY July-October 2023 was chosen carefully, as it fooled me (and most professionals, if you can get them to admit it!).

If you have closed short positions and the market is still sliding, it is good practice to set a stop order to reopen a short position a few points below the low. Again, this is personal preference managing risk. Some traders will hedge only 50% at this point as there may be a quick test of the new low and a potential bounce. The 50% hedge is effective where if you guessed wrong, you still capture some of the upside reversal.

A pro tip is if you see a new low with high volume or from a big gap, don’t sit around waiting for a bounce because you are probably in the middle of another leg down.

In general, these steps can be repeated and it’s basic. You would be surprised how many people don’t use stops for protection and end up wrecked in a large selloff.

Strategy #3: Options Contracts

Using options to hedge is not that difficult in concept, but the leverage risk and time decay impact on value might turn off some investors. Let’s not rehash the dangers of options spoken of by many. This article is about hedging, and I will assume you have some understanding about “The Greeks”. There are some differences to highlight in comparison with ETFs and Futures.

How Options Work:

  • Options allow you to control 100 shares per contract. This is helpful if you have a large number of shares of various stocks and indexes in your portfolio.

  • When volatility is low (VIX), the cost of an options contract is also low. Volatility will be low in a sideways or topping market. This indicates complacency and usually signals the calm before the storm. Once trading volume trails off on a rally, that’s the sweet spot to add cheap protection.

  • Essentially, you are paying for two things. The right to “put” your shares somewhere else that does not concern you once a selloff is over. Also, the amount of time you think it will take for the move to complete and sell your options with enough time to pocket nice profits.

  • Time is your best friend as a seller, and your worst enemy as a buyer. Time is everything in the options market. There are ways to mitigate this daily decay in value, but that is not within the scope of this article.

  • Your losses are limited to the total cost of the contract.

  • The longer the time horizon you pay for, the less time decay you will experience each day. However, the delta will be less for each point move. Longer-term contracts return less, just like 0DTE options are like lottery tickets.

Options Hedging Tips & Tricks

  • Get in, then get out with short profits. In an uptrend, markets don’t consolidate at lows for long. Once the trend flips, the inverse will be true as well. There is a reason why people say “sell the rip, buy the dip”. If you sell too early, you can always hedge again with the steps above.

  • If you have enough discipline to hold puts for a while, that is the best way to prevent over-trading.

  • Give yourself enough TIME when adding protection, 30–45 days is appropriate for short-term plays. Longer term, 60–90 days is best practice.

  • Time decay is a formidable force to deal with. If you have a sizable portfolio with a large put position, the time decay each day can be nauseating. If you enter while things are calm, the decay will be much less.

  • Buy protection while the VIX is low. No, markets don’t always go up, despite what all your friends say. The signal is declining overall volume on a long-term rally. This chart shows a divergence between price and volume. Does this look familiar? Might want to add some protection… (not advice!).

Strategy #4: Futures

Hedging with futures can be very profitable, but also scary as hell. Those of you who have lost thousands during a short bathroom break know what I’m talking about. Or, that one work meeting you said you were going to pay attention to and not trade (oops, that meeting just cost you half a paycheck - Been there, done that buddy!). Mini contracts control large amounts of notional value of underlying stock and are typically used to hedge large portfolios.

How Futures Work

  • Futures are bought and sold using 100% broker margin. That means you are borrowing money to trade a heavily leveraged derivative. Just like taking out a mortgage with the absolute minimum down payment.

  • You need cash in your account as collateral. The broker margin is only to control the contract. Once closed, any profit or loss will be adjusted from your pile of cold hard cash immediately.

  • During the normal market trading session, usually the margin cost of the contract is half of the normal margin requirement with Schwab/E*TRADE. Or, can be very low using a specialty futures broker like Tradovate. Every broker is different.

  • There is no time decay like in options, but there is an expiration date that you need to be very aware of.

  • Futures contracts that are about to expire need to be rolled into the next contract, which becomes the front month contract. Futures have specific contract periods and have letters associated with contract expiration dates.

  • Depending on the underlying asset, they can have varying degrees of contract rolls per year.

    • Example: Nasdaq has 4 contracts that cycle each year.

      • H = March

      • M = June

      • U = September

      • Z = December

The unique characteristics of futures have some pros & cons associated with it. It’s important to understand them before committing to using futures as a hedge.

Pros

  • Settled with cash, no waiting for trade date +1 for settlement of funds.

  • Very liquid, it’s almost instantaneous getting in and out of a trade.

  • Futures are taxed using the 60/40 rule: 60% are taxed at the long-term capital gains tax rate of 20%, while only 40% of your short-term capital gains are taxed at your ordinary income tax rate.

  • You can make a lot of money quickly.

  • Futures benefit from cross-margining. If you have never heard the term before, that means if you are long the front month contract, and short the back month and equal amount, your margin requirement is almost zero.

  • Most brokers will either sell you out on the last day before the front month contract rolls or call you so you don’t get assigned 1,000 bushels of wheat dropped off at your front door step. Try to explain that to your spouse.

Cons

  • Potentially, a call from the margin man. If you are on a first name basis with the four futures desk reps at E*TRADE for maintenance calls, you should consider switching professions.

  • A dip below the margin requirement per contract will mean you need to ADD funds. Ever see the movie Trading Places where the Duke brothers lose everything on an orange juice trade gone wrong? It can happen. If you are overallocated on positions and don’t have much capital to work with, this is where it will matter.

  • If you have one broker, they will not allow you to go long and short with the same margin/futures account. You will need to hedge with a back month contract. The spread can be very wide. However, that’s why the experts only use them (front month contract) for hedges, since they have a large long portfolio as the opposite side of the trade.

  • An itchy trading finger can cost you quickly.

  • Some brokers don’t let you hedge a losing trade if you have an active maintenance margin call. Even if you are only 18 dollars short. You will need to sell out/reduce position size to properly margin your account before the end of the day. That can be a major bummer if you only have cash for ONE mini contract, now you have to buy nine micro contracts and realize the loss or gain. Those trading costs add up, believe me.

  • Futures can be addictive for traders who like speed, leverage, and risk.

Conclusion

Managing your risk is of paramount importance to keep your portfolio intact while markets navigate troubled waters. Do not fear hedge positions that go awry or that are poorly timed. The market often gives second and even third chances to exit any position over time. ETFs give you downside protection without the hassle of worrying about the variable of time. However, options and futures contracts have a solid place in the hedging strategy tool belt for the veteran investor. If you mess up a hedge (and you will fat finger an order at least once!) take a deep breath, relax, and make a plan to get back to green. Remember that patience = profit.

If this article has been helpful, please let me know in the comments! I hope you have enjoyed my first article and look forward to many more.

data.title: Unlocking Stock Market Success: Why You Should Embrace The Skew
data.description:
By C. Thomas Howard

When we talk about stock returns, most people assume that individual stocks should yield positive returns. That’s because the stock market has historically outperformed other asset classes like bonds. But surprisingly, the median monthly return for a large sample of individual stocks is - drumroll, please – zero. That’s right. A study conducted by Henric Bessembinder and published in the Financial Analysts Journal in April 2023 found that on a monthly basis, individual stocks generate returns centered around zero. In fact, this paints a “half-full, half-empty” scenario. Half the stocks produce positive returns, while the other half have negative returns.

As an investor or advisor, how do you and your clients react to this? If this zero-median return statistic were the only way to look at stock performance, it would be hard to justify investing in stocks at all. Convincing clients to invest in equities would be an uphill battle, especially if they’re seeking short-term gains.

Volatility

In fact, there are many ways to evaluate stock returns beyond just focusing on median monthly performance. One common approach is to measure stock returns in terms of volatility. Volatility refers to how much a stock’s price fluctuates, and it’s often measured using standard deviation. On average, the annual standard deviation for stock returns is about 50%, which means that the price of an individual stock can swing wildly throughout the year. If we apply the 95% confidence interval often used in statistics, this implies that an individual stock’s return could vary by roughly +/- 100% in a given year. This is huge. Essentially, an individual stock could double or lose all its value within 12 months.

This level of uncertainty can make stocks seem daunting, especially for those looking for stability. The idea that individual stocks are a “half-full, half-empty” proposition monthly, and are even more volatile annually, can scare away potential investors. But it’s important to remember that stocks are primarily intended to be long-term investments.

The short-term ups and downs, while nerve-wracking, are part of the journey toward long-term wealth creation.

So, what happens when we shift our focus to long-term individual stock returns? Shouldn’t we expect more consistency over time? Bessembinder also looked at long-term stock performance, and the findings weren’t exactly comforting. Over the long run, 55% of US stocks underperformed US Treasury Bill returns, meaning that more than half of individual stocks did worse than the safest government-backed investments. Perhaps even more alarming is the fact that the most common outcome for individual stocks was a 100% loss - complete failure. These findings suggest that investing in individual stocks is a high-risk endeavor, even when taking a long-term approach.

Typically, when investors and financial analysts assess stock performance, they focus on two key statistical measures: central value (such as the mean or median return) and volatility (as measured by standard deviation). This traditional method of analysis often leads to a negative or at least discouraging narrative about investing in individual stocks.

If returns are largely zero in the short term, highly volatile in the medium term, and risky in the long term, why would anyone invest in stocks?

The answer, as history shows, is that despite these challenges, stocks have significantly outperformed other asset classes like bonds and cash over extended periods. But to truly understand why, we need to look beyond the typical first two parameters used in analyzing stock returns.

The Third Parameter for Assessing Stock Performance: Positive Skew

While traditional analysis focuses heavily on the first two parameters - central value and volatility - it misses a crucial component of stock returns: positive skew. Positive skew is the third parameter of stock return distribution, and it’s key to explaining why stocks have historically outperformed other investments. If we only focus on central value and volatility, we are essentially assuming that stock returns follow a normal distribution, similar to a bell curve. This assumption works well for many natural phenomena, but it doesn’t apply to stock returns.

Why not? Because stock returns are not governed by natural laws; they are driven by the actions of human beings, who are often irrational and driven by emotions. Unlike natural events that follow predictable patterns, stock prices are the result of complex human behaviors - fear, greed, speculation, optimism, and panic. This emotional backdrop means that stock prices can shoot up dramatically when crowds get carried away but can only drop to a limit of -100% (when a stock loses all its value). This is what creates a positive skew in stock returns.

In simple terms, while the downside for any stock is capped at a 100% loss, the upside is theoretically unlimited. An investor might lose all their money on one stock, but another stock could skyrocket, gaining 200%, 500%, or even more.

It is this asymmetry in returns –the fact that the gains can far exceed the losses - that generates positive skew.

This skew, combined with the magic of multi-period compounding, explains much of the long-term value of investing in stocks.

Learn to Tolerate Tail Events

If you examine stock return distributions, you’ll notice that the long-term value from investing in the market comes primarily from tail events. These are the rare but extreme outcomes that occur at both ends of the distribution. The long, positive tail is what produces the outsized returns that more than make up for the smaller, frequent losses. For stocks to have generated the high returns we’ve seen historically, the large positive tail events must have outweighed the large negative ones.

The more positively skewed the return distribution, the higher the long-term returns.

This might sound counterintuitive at first, especially when traditional portfolio management strategies focus on eliminating volatility. Portfolio construction discussions often center around how to smooth out the ride by reducing exposure to extreme events, both positive and negative.

The goal is to create a more-predictable and less-volatile return stream, which can feel safer for investors. However, in avoiding those unnerving tail events, investors eliminate both the big losses and the big gains. This reduces positive skew and, as a result, dramatically reduces overall returns.

The Hidden Cost of Managed Equity

A typical “Managed Equity” strategy eliminates all stock losses (no returns less than zero) while capping upside returns. For example, a well-known investment company offers a managed S&P 500 fund that avoids all annual losses while limiting returns to less than 7%. Since it is virtually impossible to predict daily returns, this return feat is accomplished by simply holding a zero cost S&P 500 options collar. Over the last 40+ years, when the S&P 500 generated more than 11% annually, this strategy would have yielded a meager 4% annual return.

In other words, avoiding emotional tail events means you miss out on the very returns that are the major drivers of long-term wealth creation. Investors who focus too much on smoothing returns end up with more consistent but dramatically lower returns over time.

To truly benefit from stock investing, it’s necessary to embrace both the emotions and the rewards that come with positive skew. This means learning to live with tail events. They may be uncomfortable when they occur, but they are an integral part of long-term success in the stock market.

The most successful investors recognize this and accept that volatility and tail events that are simply unavoidable are crucial for achieving high returns. By learning to appreciate positive skew and its associated tail events, investors can unlock the full potential of stock market gains.

Learn to love, not fear the skew.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BCX: High Fees And Underperformance
data.description:
Introduction

The typical advice given for buy and hold investors looking for a diversified portfolio would be to purchase a low-cost broad market index fund like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). This has worked out tremendously over the past few decades, yielding well over 10%+ returns compounded every year depending on the entry point.

It might be reasonable to ask if this stunning performance will continue, especially as the tailwinds of falling interest rates, falling corporate tax rates, and expanding government budget deficits may not be significant factors moving forward.

There is also a concern that a handful of large tech companies have come to dominate too greatly, representing almost a third of the index on their own. Is the S&P 500 truly diversified? Energy and materials are as important to the economy and modern life as any other sector, and yet they combined make up less than 6% of the index.

BlackRock's Answer: BCX

An investor looking to diversify away from the S&P 500's tech dominance might reasonably choose to focus a greater percentage of their portfolio on energy and materials. BlackRock (BLK) targets these investors with their Resources & Commodities Strategy Trust (NYSE:BCX), an actively managed closed-end fund that can fill this diversification role in a portfolio. But is it the best option?

BCX focuses on companies that mine natural resources and produce commodities. It does not utilize leverage, but does utilize covered call options to enhance current income. The distribution currently sits around a healthy 6.6%, but the expense ratio is a hefty 1.06%. It typically trades around a 10% discount to its net asset value, and only rarely trades at a premium:

It holds 46 positions, with about one third in energy companies, one quarter in agriculture companies, and the rest in mining and materials. The top ten holdings represent about half of the total assets, and give a good picture of the fund's strategy:

Shell (SHEL), Exxon (XOM) and Total (TTE) are large multinational energy companies, and Rio Tinto (RIO), Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF), and Teck (TECK) are large diversified mining companies. Gold, fertilizer, and aluminum are also represented. The focus here is on large, diversified companies within the fund's core sectors.

To look under the hood at the full list of holdings, we have to consult the semi-annual report. I was curious to find that within the "agriculture" sector is actually a large 9.8% allocation to packaging, also represented in the top ten holdings by Smurfit Westrock Plc (OTCPK:SMFTF). This is an odd move to me, as I do not think of packaging as a natural resource in the same way as energy or metals.

Performance

Here is where things for BCX start to look less appealing. I find the easiest benchmarks for natural resources to be the price of gold itself, represented by the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) and the energy sector as a whole, represented by The Energy Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLE). Since BCX's inception, here are the results:

Surprisingly, XLE and GLD have very similar returns over this time period, while BCX managed to return less than half of the basic ETF counterparts. Time frames can be somewhat arbitrary, however, so here is a 5-year chart:

BCX kept up with GLD during this time period, but noticeably lags XLE. BCX charges over 1% per year in fees, while XLE (0.09% expense ratio) and GLD (0.4% expense ratio) have much simpler structures that allow them to be cheaper to hold. Since BCX does not seem to outperform them convincingly, I tend to prefer the cheaper and simpler investments.

When it comes to individual tickers, the disconnect with BCX becomes even clearer.

Franco-Nevada (FNV) is the largest and most diversified precious metals royalty/streaming company, a much safer business model than mining. They are exposed heavily to copper and energy as well as gold, making it perfect for exposure to natural resources. It is a safe buy and hold type of stock that I have written on in the past, and it has almost 10x the return of BCX since inception.

CF Industries (CF) is my go-to idea of a fertilizer company, since they are US-based to take advantage of cheap natural gas feedstock and they focus on the more lucrative nitrogen fertilizes, as opposed to the more readily available potash or phosphates. Again, they have about 10x the return of BCX.

Lastly I included Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), since BCX may appeal to investors looking for safe income based on natural resources. In that case, EPD provides a much higher distribution and total return, as it is a particularly well run company.

I mentioned packaging, and the stock that immediately comes to my mind is the industry leader Packaging Corporation of America (PKG). They deserve their own chart:

The difference in return here is nothing short of astronomical.

Similarly, coal has had a massive run in the last few years, as investors in natural resources should be aware of. It seems that BCX missed that train as well:

I know that I am cherry-picking certain successful companies in BCX's sectors over the last decade, but the point is that successful investments were there, and BCX failed to benefit meaningfully from them. These are also some of the most central leaders in their respective industries and thus make for reasonable benchmarks. The value proposition of BCX, then, can be difficult to see.

Covered Calls

Part of the structural issue here is that selling covered calls tends to magnify the disposition effect, where the best stocks in the portfolio are called away, leaving the underperformers to remain in the portfolio. This effect is present in many instruments that utilize covered calls, and the concept can be illustrated by comparing the performance of SPY to the Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD):

Covered calls fail to provide significant enough downside protection to actually prevent large drawdowns, and then they slow the inevitable bounce back by capping upside. They thrive in sideways markets, which is a very specific situation. In the long run, buy and hold is typically a better strategy, so selling covered calls is a form of market-timing that adds a layer of difficulty that often results in underperformance.

Index-tracking ETFs tend to have the exact opposite tendencies, since their weightings are based on market cap. Winners rise to the top and become higher weights in the index, while losers grow smaller and receive less of an allocation.

Risks

To the downside, the covered call strategy is a risk in itself, as it often leads to underperformance when compared to buy and hold.

The more apparent risk with BCX, however, is active management risk. Every year, investors pay management 1% of their investment in order to hopefully outperform passive benchmarks like XLE or GLD. However, this has not been the case throughout BCX's history.

The sectors BCX is involved with themselves are highly risky. Energy has great geopolitical risk, especially with the importance and volatility of the Middle East to energy markets.

Mining also has jurisdictional risk, with companies operating in several countries, each with their own regulations and tax codes. Natural disasters can wipe out whole mining operations, and irresponsible mining and harm the environment in ways that are difficult for companies to rebound from.

Further, miners and producers have a paradoxical relationship to inflation. Inflation increases the price of commodities like gold that are being mined. However, it also increases the cost of labor, infrastructure and equipment, and the energy used to mine. So while natural resources should be a hedge against inflation, mining in practice can have many variables that limit its hedging value.

That said, my thesis is that investors should not own BCX, and thus there are upside risks to consider as well.

As mentioned, covered calls can provide income in low-volatility sideways markets. If we do encounter that situation, then BCX might well outperform passive benchmarks that do not benefit from this additional income generation.

Further, past performance is not indicative of future results. The fact that BCX has underperformed in the past does not necessarily mean that they will continue to underperform in the future. Investors who believe that BlackRock's managers have a valuable edge may well be proved correct if BCX begins to select winning stocks for their portfolio.

Lastly, a bull market in such sectors as agriculture and packaging may not be captured fully in XLE or GLD or any other passive index. BCX may be a large beneficiary of such a bull market, leading to outperformance of my alternatives.

Recommendation

Putting this all together, for investors who are looking to buy and hold passive exposure to mining and natural resources, I recommend sticking with the basics and allocating to XLE and GLD. They will provide the desired diversification away from the expensive and tech-heavy S&P 500 and are ETFs you can buy and forget about. Not only have they significantly outperformed BCX in the long run, but they also do not have the manager risk or the covered call risk or the over 1% expense ratio in fees.

For the more active investor, it is worth finding a few high-quality stocks in these sectors to maintain exposure. I have already mentioned two. Franco Nevada (FNV) has a safe and reliable royalty/streaming model based primarily on gold, copper, and energy. Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) also has a safe pipeline model to benefit from expansion in oil, gas, and refined products, and has beaten BCX as well. These are two very high-quality companies that one can buy and hold and check up on periodically. Seeking Alpha provides extensive coverage on these and other tickers that might remain within the mining and natural resources sectors and outperform BCX moving forward.

To conclude, BCX provides diversified exposure to materials and energy sectors that may be desirable. However, due to its underperformance, I do not believe that investors are adequately compensated for the risk of active management. There are options out there that, I think, would suit most strategies better, and so I assign a sell rating in favor of other ideas.

data.title: Winnebago Industries, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Winnebago Industries, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Verizon's Disappointing Earnings Don't Mean Its Overvalued
data.description:
Verizon Communications Inc. (NYSE:VZ), one of the largest telecommunication firms with a market capitalization of around $175 billion, recently announced its earnings. The company continues to have an impressive dividend of more than 6% that it can comfortably afford. We've discussed the company before as an investment we're a huge fan of as a result of its FCF and returns.

However, despite all the company's discussions about growth, its financials have stagnated, which presents a major risk.

Verizon Business Momentum

Verizon is focused on seeing continued success from its business, with 4.2 million FWA subscribers, its continued goal.

The company's primary shareholder return is its dividend with a 6.5% yield after the market reacted poorly to the company's earnings, pushing its share price towards $40 / share. The company can afford that dividend comfortably with its FCF. However, it needs to be able to provide shareholder returns past solely dividends.

The company is working through a number of strategic moves such as its acquisition of Frontier, but that deal will take almost 2 years to close.

Verizon Operating Metrics

The company's operating metrics show continued volatility across its business segments with strength in broadband.

The company saw 2.7 million wireless retail postpaid phone gross adds, with net adds much weaker. Consumer retail postpaid phone net adds were less than 100K, however, the company's retail net adds were stronger. The company's broadband net adds have been trending downwards, but still hit almost 400K.

The company's fiber optic segment remains relatively weak, with 43K net adds, however, the company's acquisition of Frontier should help.

Verizon Financials

Financially, the company's revenue was roughly flat YoY with $33.3 billion in revenue in both quarters.

The company managed to increase adjusted EBITDA by 2% YoY, roughly in line with inflation and showing an ability to continue performing with stagnant revenue. The company did see a slight decline in its adjusted EPS and the company's wireless service revenue continues to remain a core of the company's business at strength.

With weakness in the company's share price, it's back to a single-digit P/E based on its adjusted EPS.

Financially, the company's CFFO declined by $2.3 billion YoY for the trailing 9-months. That's incredibly disappointing to see financially. However, the company did manage to cut capital expenditures by $2.2 billion as 5G and associated expenses went down. At the end of the day, the company managed to see $14.5 billion in FCF for the 9M ending 2024, down <1% YoY.

Annualized, the company's FCF is almost $20 billion, giving the company an FCF of ~11.5%. That's a relatively lofty FCF yield, that enables the company's growing dividend of $8.4 billion. The company's total debt went up YoY with net unsecured debt to adjusted EBITDA down from 2.6x to 2.5x as the company's EBITDA increased slightly.

The company's incredibly lofty debt yield costs the company more than $5 billion in annual interest expenditures. While rates have peaked and the company's financial strength isn't a concern in terms of handling the debt, it certainly doesn't have the room to let that debt increase further. At the same time, investor concern has continued to put pressure on the company.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that Verizon's financial growth is slowing down. The company has a massive debt load costing it billions in annual interest expenditures and FCF is approximately flat. That will hurt the company's ability to continue driving long-term shareholder returns, making it a poor investment for shareholders.

Conclusion

Verizon has an impressive portfolio of assets where the company's cell phone business makes up its core. The company is working to expand this mobility business while expanding its fiber business substantially. The company's ability to close its acquisition of Frontier will be a major support for the company here.

Going forward the company has a lofty debt load of roughly $150 billion. The company needs to be able to handle that debt load, however, with reducing interest rates, it's a manageable concern. The biggest aspects for the company are if it can continue increasing its dividend, with 18 years of growth and a 6.5% yield, to generate long-term returns.

Please let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Dominion Energy's Deal With Amazon Is A Major Step In Nuclear Energy
data.description:
There is no hotter topic of conversation than artificial intelligence. The extraordinary boom in AI technologies is transforming industries. Emerging technologies like Generative AI have transformative applications across industries from drug development to financial modeling. This new demand for AI models, particularly large language models, is catalyzing significant changes in the infrastructure required to support computing.

One consequence of this emerging revolution is the increased electricity demand and the need for modernized data centers capable of supporting generative AI. We recently published an article detailing the rise of AI and the corresponding increase in electricity demand. The article detailed the elevated electricity usage by large language models, such as ChatGPT.

Both public and corporate imaginations were triggered by the release of OpenAI's ChatGPT on November 30, 2022. Evidence about how widely these tools will be used and how much they will change computational needs is just starting to emerge. These early applications were estimated to require about ten times the electricity-from 0.3 watt-hours for a traditional Google search to 2.9 watt-hours for a Chat-GPT query-to respond to user queries. The creation of original music, photos, and videos based on user prompts and other emerging AI applications could require much more power.

The infrastructure supporting generative AI is housed in large data centers. Chipsets from companies like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) fill these enormous facilities that require extraordinary amounts of power. These facilities are equipped with specialized hardware like GPUs and TPUs designed to process the underlying language used by these models. Over the last few years, companies like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) have significantly expanded their data center footprints. Amazon Web Services is a leader in AI investment, including their $50 million Generative AI initiative.

The rapid rise of generative AI is changing the world, but it is also straining the world's electricity production. Given we are in the early innings, there is work to be done if we are to support the future of AI. Data centers are being developed at an unprecedented rate to meet these needs, accounting for a large portion of the surge in electricity consumption.

Today, we revisit one of the greatest secondary beneficiaries of the AI revolution. Dominion Energy, Inc. (NYSE:D) is a regional energy provider serving the East Coast. In August, we assigned a Strong Buy rating, exploring the various tailwinds of AI and how D was proactively working to meet new demand. This could be a turning point for D as it emerges from the troubles of the rising interest rate era.

Recent Performance

Since our previous coverage of D, the stock has performed well. Broadly, tailwinds such as rate cuts have benefitted interest rate sensitive sectors, utilities included. Since the beginning of August, D has climbed alongside the utilities sector (XLU), trailing the sector slightly. However, D has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) by approximately 200 basis points.

With more rate cuts on the horizon, additional enthusiasm continues to grow around companies like D. As the financing environment becomes more accommodative, additional tailwinds have begun to grow in favor of D.

Who Is Dominion Energy?

Our last coverage provided a detailed summary of D, including the business model and recent corporate developments. We also discussed D's valuation, dividend history, and risk factors that would impact long-term performance. For brevity, today's conversation will focus on recent corporate developments, but below is a summary of D's business from our prior coverage.

D produces and distributes power in two primary markets, Virginia and South Carolina. Despite the limited geography, D is one of the largest utility providers in the nation in terms of generation/delivery and market capitalization. D has three primary business units, Dominion Energy Virginia, Dominion Energy South Carolina, and their smaller Contracted Energy segment.

The Dominion Energy Virginia unit delivers electricity to nearly three million customers in Virginia and North Carolina. The Dominion Energy South Carolina unit is a provider of power for around 800,000 customers in South Carolina. This segment also delivers natural gas to a smaller number of customers in South Carolina. The Contracted Energy unit is involved in renewable electricity and natural gas.

At the end of 2023, D had nearly 30 gigawatts of peak generation capacity and corresponding delivery infrastructure. D is one of the largest utility companies in the United States in terms of customer base and capacity.

As one of the largest electricity providers in the United States, D plays a critical role in meeting electricity demand. The company is also one of the oldest utility providers and operates nuclear power plants. Nuclear energy has returned to public attention recently. Even more recently, nuclear has been proposed as a viable solution for the increasing power demand. Recently, officials announced that Three Mile Island was going to begin renovations in just three months to support MSFT's AI aspirations.

Constellation announced last month that it would revive the half-century-old Three Mile Island with the purpose of fueling Microsoft's data centers. Microsoft is expected to pay at least $100 a megawatt-hour, nearly double the typical cost of renewable energy in the region, as part of the 20-year power contract.

This example highlights the extreme demand for electricity as AI begins to get a foothold in the mainstream business world.

According to the IEA, the demand for electricity has already begun to accelerate at the fastest rate in decades. Data from The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) suggests that data center electricity demand will grow by nearly 15% annually through the end of the decade.

But Why Virginia?

Loudoun County, VA has more data center capacity than the next five largest markets combined. Loudoun County houses around 50% of the nation's total data center capacity. The region was the origin of the internet and acts as a thoroughfare for digital infrastructure. Additionally, power availability has made this geography one of the most attractive for new data centers. Proximity to national security resources such as the Pentagon would not hurt the region's desirability, either. Not to mention proximity to the nation's capital.

Data from S&P indicates that more than 80 new data centers have finished and connected to D's infrastructure over the past five years. The electricity demand associated with this is enormous.

What Has Changed?

As technology companies become increasingly hungry for electricity, the game is changing. Rather than rely on municipalities and the states to provide the necessary infrastructure to support their operations, some of these behemoths have turned to direct partnerships. We mentioned earlier that MSFT is pursuing a partnership with Three Mile Island and Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG).

Recently, AMZN announced a new initiative to support carbon-free operations. The main piece of this puzzle will be AMZN's partnership in developing small nuclear reactors, called Small Modular Reactors or SMRs. One of these agreements was directly with D to develop an SMR alongside the company's North Anna nuclear power station. In a press release, AMZN detailed the partnership.

In Virginia, we've signed an agreement with utility company Dominion Energy to explore the development of an SMR project near Dominion's existing North Anna nuclear power station. This will bring at least 300 megawatts of power to the Virginia region, where Dominion projects that power demands will increase by 85% over the next 15 years.

AMZN is taking a multipronged approach across the country to create a diversified network of power demand that will meet their needs. Projecting that electricity needs in Virginia alone are likely to double over the next 15 years, companies like D are working to meet this coming demand. While D is currently exploring green energy solutions such as wind, nuclear appears to be the only feasible solution to actually meet the forecasted demand.

D provided additional color about their agreement with AMZN. CEO Robert M Blue provided commentary on the partnership.

This agreement builds on our longstanding partnership with Amazon and other leading tech companies to accelerate the development of carbon-free power generation in Virginia," said Robert M. Blue, Chair, President and CEO of Dominion Energy. "It's an important step forward in serving our customers' growing needs with reliable, affordable and increasingly clean energy. This collaboration gives us a potential path to advance SMRs with minimal rate impacts for our residential customers and substantially reduced development risk.

Why Amazon Deal Is Important?

The past several months have produced several important developments in the AI power demand story. As MSFT forms their strategic partnership to power their AI business, AMZN has taken similar steps by forming a relationship with D. For D, securing a relationship with a Magnificent 7 company is an entry into the world of big tech.

The partnership is a monumental step for D and an important message to naysayers who believed D was unable to meet upcoming demand. Perhaps more importantly, this continues D's shifts towards being a green energy provider. Combined with their offshore wind projects, D is leading the way in alternative energy that will also meet the demands of the future. The partnership will help AMZN reach their goal of a zero-carbon operation by 2040. Nuclear energy has been reiterated as a critical piece of this goal.

The development of advanced nuclear technologies is a long time coming. Other solutions such as Sam Altman's Okta, Inc. (OKTA) are proposing similarly scaled nuclear reactors that are safe, reliable, and significantly cheaper than large-scale plants. During the summer, D requested proposals for SMR technology to assess the feasibility of modifying the North Anna nuclear plant.

In July, Dominion Energy announced a Request for Proposals (RFP) from leading SMR nuclear technology companies to evaluate the feasibility of developing an SMR at the company's North Anna Power Station in Louisa County, Va. While the RFP is not a commitment to build an SMR at North Anna, it is an important first step in evaluating the technology and the feasibility of developing it at North Anna.

This importantly signals initiative towards nuclear technology. AMZN is planning to invest $35 billion in new data center campuses across D's markets. These data centers will go online and connect to D's network, creating extraordinary new demand for the company. As they get ahead of the game, partnering with D directly marks a significant shift from D's traditional business model. AMZN's total investment of around $500 million into nuclear energy is an extraordinary opportunity for a partner like D.

Conclusion

Over the coming years, D's demand drivers are almost certain to continue firing on all cylinders. We will continue to monitor exciting developments across the energy sector as power-hungry data centers continue to be built across the country. With a circle of support for new infrastructure coming from the private sector and elected officials, companies like D will continue to benefit. Even Governor Youngkin of Virginia commented on the deal's benefits.

In alignment with our All-American, All-of-the-Above energy plan, small modular nuclear reactors will play a critical role in positioning Virginia as a leading nuclear innovation hub. Amazon Web Services' commitment to this technology and their partnership with Dominion is a significant step forward to meet the future power needs of a growing Virginia.

Partnerships with companies like AMZN have already begun to materialize and will almost certainly continue. In fact, the proliferation of this direct relationship model could begin if these initial forays are successful.

Following several months of exceptional performance and price appreciation, we are adjusting D to a Buy rating. The AI opportunity is too big to ignore. Watching companies like D race to capitalize on the opportunity set has already yielded results.
